label: Standard — B2C SaaS, pre-MVP

intake.product_description: A personal finance tracking app that automatically categorizes expenses from bank statements and credit cards, then generates weekly spending summaries with actionable recommendations to help users reduce unnecessary spending.
intake.target_user: Salaried professionals aged 25–35 who are trying to build savings but struggle to track where their money goes each month and feel overwhelmed by manual budgeting tools.
intake.research_goal: Understand what specific friction points prevent salaried professionals from sticking with budgeting tools, and what types of automated insights would actually change their spending behavior.

notes: Control case. Well-specified inputs, common domain, clear research goal. All agents should perform at ceiling. Baseline for regression detection.

label: Standard — B2B productivity tool, MVP

intake.product_description: A project management tool for remote engineering teams that automatically surfaces blockers from Slack conversations and GitHub activity, then creates a daily digest showing which tasks are at risk of missing sprint deadlines.
intake.target_user: Engineering managers at startups with 10–50 person teams who spend excessive time in standups and status-check meetings and want automated visibility into team progress without micromanaging.
intake.research_goal: Identify the specific information that engineering managers need to feel confident about sprint health, and what friction in existing tools causes them to fall back on synchronous standups.

notes: Control case. B2B context, technical audience. Tests whether A1 can generate diverse manager archetypes (skeptic, efficiency-focused, process-heavy, hands-off, etc.).

label: Standard — Consumer wellness, idea stage

intake.product_description: A sleep improvement app that uses nightly check-in data and wearable sensor data to identify patterns in what disrupts sleep quality, then delivers a personalized one-action recommendation each morning to improve the following night.
intake.target_user: Adults aged 30–45 with diagnosed or self-identified sleep issues who have tried multiple sleep apps and supplements but haven't found a consistent solution and are frustrated by generic advice.
intake.research_goal: Understand how people with persistent sleep problems currently try to diagnose and fix their sleep, what has failed for them, and what would make a personalized recommendation feel credible enough to act on.

notes: Control case. Consumer health domain, idea stage. Tests A4 synthesis of emotionally nuanced transcripts where personas have tried-and-failed histories.

label: Domain complexity — HealthTech / HIPAA

intake.product_description: A HIPAA-compliant clinical documentation platform for independent primary care physicians that uses voice recognition to auto-populate SOAP notes during patient visits, reducing post-visit charting time from 30 minutes to under 5 minutes.
intake.target_user: Independent primary care physicians running solo or small group practices who spend 2–3 hours daily on post-visit documentation, leading to burnout, late departures, and reduced time with family. They are comfortable with EHRs but skeptical of AI-generated clinical notes.
intake.research_goal: Understand the specific steps in the current clinical documentation workflow where time is lost, what accuracy and liability concerns would block adoption of AI-generated SOAP notes, and what level of physician control over final notes is non-negotiable.

notes: Medium difficulty. Highly regulated domain with real liability stakes. A1 must generate skeptical, liability-conscious physician personas — not just efficiency-seekers. A3 responses should include domain-specific language and clinical context. A4 must not generalize regulatory concerns into vague 'compliance is important' themes.

label: Domain complexity — EdTech / K12 teachers

intake.product_description: An adaptive quiz generation tool for K–12 teachers that ingests lesson plan PDFs and learning objectives, then generates differentiated quizzes with Bloom's Taxonomy aligned questions at three difficulty tiers, exportable to Google Classroom.
intake.target_user: K–12 public school teachers in the US who have 20–35 students per class with mixed learning levels, spend significant time creating assessment materials, and are required to demonstrate differentiated instruction in their lesson plans but lack time to implement it consistently.
intake.research_goal: Understand what makes differentiated assessment creation painful enough that teachers skip it, what level of control teachers need to feel ownership over AI-generated questions, and what integration with existing school tech stacks would be required for adoption.

notes: Medium difficulty. Public sector constraints (budget, IT approval, time poverty) must surface in personas. A1 should generate range from tech-enthusiastic early adopter to resistant veteran teacher. A3 responses should reflect classroom realities, not idealized teacher behavior.

label: Domain complexity — LegalTech / contract review

intake.product_description: An AI-powered contract review tool for in-house legal teams at mid-market companies that automatically flags non-standard clauses, liability gaps, and missing representations in NDAs, vendor agreements, and employment contracts, with suggested redline language.
intake.target_user: In-house legal counsel and legal operations managers at companies with 200–2000 employees who review 50–200 contracts per month without sufficient headcount, rely heavily on outside counsel for complex review, and are under pressure from business teams to turn contracts around in under 24 hours.
intake.research_goal: Understand the specific contract types and clause categories where AI-assisted review would reduce legal risk exposure, what trust threshold in-house teams need before relying on AI redline suggestions for business-critical contracts, and what integration with existing contract lifecycle management tools is required.

notes: Medium difficulty. High-stakes professional domain. A3 must produce legally-contextualized responses — generic 'I want automation to save time' responses are a failure mode. A4 must surface trust-building and liability concerns as separate themes, not merge them.

label: Domain complexity — B2B Enterprise SaaS

intake.product_description: An enterprise procurement intelligence platform that aggregates vendor pricing data, contract renewal dates, and spend analytics across business units, giving CPOs and procurement directors a single dashboard to identify consolidation opportunities and negotiate better vendor terms.
intake.target_user: CPOs and VP-level procurement directors at enterprises with 1000–10000 employees who manage $50M+ in annual vendor spend across 200+ vendors, currently operating with fragmented spreadsheets and disconnected ERP data, and are measured on savings generated per quarter.
intake.research_goal: Understand how procurement leaders currently identify and act on vendor consolidation opportunities, what data gaps prevent confident negotiation, and what would make a new procurement intelligence tool worth the integration effort given their existing investment in SAP and Oracle systems.

notes: Medium difficulty. Enterprise B2B with complex buying committee and political dynamics. A1 must generate personas differentiated by organizational maturity and political appetite for change — not just seniority level.

label: Input stress — vague research goal

intake.product_description: A mobile app that helps freelancers manage their projects, clients, invoices, and time tracking in one place. The app sends reminders for overdue invoices and upcoming deadlines and provides a dashboard showing monthly revenue and active project count.
intake.target_user: Independent freelancers across design, development, writing, and consulting disciplines who juggle multiple clients simultaneously and find it difficult to stay on top of their business administration while doing the actual work they were hired to do.
intake.research_goal: Learn more about what freelancers want from project management tools and understand their pain points better so we can improve the product.

notes: Hard — stress test for vague research goal. A2 must not fall back on generic 'tell me about your experience' questions — it must impose structure the intake lacks. A4 synthesis risks vague themes when questions were broad. Pipeline coherence (CP-1) is primary risk.

label: Input stress — vague target user

intake.product_description: An AI writing assistant specifically designed for scientific and academic writing that enforces APA and MLA citation formats, flags methodological inconsistencies in research sections, and suggests improvements to abstract clarity and logical argument flow. The tool integrates directly with Google Docs and Overleaf.
intake.target_user: People who write research papers and academic content who need help making their writing better and ensuring it follows the right academic standards for their field.
intake.research_goal: Understand the specific stages of academic paper writing where researchers lose the most time to formatting and structural errors, and what level of autonomous suggestion versus manual control they expect from an AI writing assistant to maintain intellectual ownership of their work.

notes: Hard — vague target user forces A1 to invent segmentation. Risk of homogeneous personas (all grad students). A3 convergence risk is high when A1 produces similar personas. Tests whether pipeline coherence survives weak A1 inputs.

label: Input stress — minimal viable input

intake.product_description: A tool that helps small restaurant owners manage their online reviews from Google, Yelp, and TripAdvisor in one dashboard, suggests AI-drafted responses to negative reviews, and tracks sentiment trends over time to identify recurring complaints.
intake.target_user: Small restaurant owners or managers who handle their own online reputation without a dedicated marketing team and feel stressed by negative reviews.
intake.research_goal: Understand how restaurant owners currently handle negative reviews and what would make them trust an AI-suggested response enough to use it.

notes: Hard — all fields at or near minimum character counts. Only 4 personas, 5 questions. Tests floor behavior: can the system produce usable output when inputs are minimal? A4 themes must still cite 2+ personas despite the small set.

label: Boundary — minimum configuration (2 personas, 4 questions)

intake.product_description: A browser extension for e-commerce shoppers that automatically finds and applies coupon codes at checkout across major retail sites, and highlights when a price on a given item is historically low based on 90-day price tracking data.
intake.target_user: Value-conscious online shoppers aged 22–40 who frequently shop at major retailers like Amazon, Target, and Best Buy and feel uncertain whether they are getting the best price at the time of purchase.
intake.research_goal: Understand what friction exists in the current checkout experience that prevents shoppers from manually searching for coupons, and what trust signals would make them comfortable letting an extension automatically apply codes on their behalf.

notes: Boundary — minimum allowed configuration. Only 2 personas means A4 themes must cite both personas to qualify (2+ unique personas requirement is borderline). A4-D3 (cross-persona contrast) is hardest at N=2.

label: Boundary — maximum configuration (8 personas, 8 questions)

intake.product_description: A comprehensive HR platform for mid-size companies that automates the entire employee lifecycle from onboarding paperwork and benefits enrollment to performance review cycles, compensation benchmarking, and offboarding checklists. The platform integrates with existing payroll systems and provides People Analytics dashboards for HR directors.
intake.target_user: HR managers and HR directors at companies with 200–500 employees who are currently managing HR processes across multiple disconnected tools including a legacy HRIS, spreadsheets for performance reviews, and manual onboarding checklists. They are evaluated on time-to-productivity for new hires, employee NPS, and reduction in administrative overhead.
intake.research_goal: Identify which specific HR lifecycle stages cause the most operational pain and compliance risk in mid-size companies, what integration requirements with payroll and benefits providers are non-negotiable for adoption, and what ROI metrics HR directors use to justify platform investments to their CFOs.

notes: Boundary — maximum configuration. 8 diverse personas must be genuinely distinct (A1-D2). 8 questions increases convergence risk for A3. A4 must synthesize across 8 personas without losing minority signals in majority themes.

label: Boundary — post-launch product with existing users

intake.product_description: A subscription-based meal planning app that is currently live with 50,000 active users. The app generates weekly meal plans based on dietary preferences, auto-generates grocery lists, and integrates with Instacart for one-click grocery ordering. The team is considering adding a social feature that lets users share meals and follow friends.
intake.target_user: Current active users of the app who have been subscribed for 3+ months, primarily health-conscious adults aged 28–45 who use the app to reduce decision fatigue around weeknight meals and reduce food waste. They have established usage patterns and opinions about what works and what doesn't.
intake.research_goal: Understand whether current power users want a social layer in a meal planning app, what specific social behaviors would add value versus feel intrusive, and what risks exist in adding social features to a currently private and utilitarian product experience.

notes: Boundary — post-launch stage. Personas should be existing power users with formed opinions, not generic first-time users. A3 responses should reflect genuine ambivalence (the feature could go either way). A4 must surface both pro and anti-social-feature camps as distinct findings, not collapse them.

label: Hard — contradictory inputs

intake.product_description: A zero-UI ambient computing assistant that passively listens to all conversations in the home, learns behavioral patterns from household members over 30 days, and proactively surfaces calendar reminders, shopping list additions, and home automation triggers without any deliberate voice commands or screen interaction.
intake.target_user: Privacy-conscious technology enthusiasts aged 30–50 who are deeply uncomfortable with always-on microphone devices, have strong opinions about data sovereignty, and actively use tools like Pi-hole, VPNs, and end-to-end encrypted messaging to minimize their digital footprint.
intake.research_goal: Understand whether privacy-focused users can be convinced to adopt a passive always-on home listening device if given sufficient transparency and control mechanisms, and what privacy guarantees would be both technically credible and sufficient to overcome their fundamental objections.

notes: Hard — the target user is fundamentally opposed to the product's core mechanism. A1 must generate skeptical-to-hostile personas; any persona expressing uncritical enthusiasm is a failure. A3 responses should surface genuine distrust, not politely-framed concerns. A4 should not synthesize this into a generic 'privacy concerns theme' — it should surface the fundamental product-user mismatch.

label: Hard — ultra-niche domain

intake.product_description: A specialized data management platform for independent taxidermy studios that digitizes specimen intake logs, tracks chemical preservative inventory with auto-reorder triggers, manages client communication timelines for multi-month projects, and generates CITES permit documentation for international specimen shipments.
intake.target_user: Professional taxidermists who run independent studios with 1–3 employees, handle 50–200 specimens per year across mammals, birds, and fish, are required to maintain CITES compliance records for protected species work, and currently manage all of this through paper logbooks, spreadsheets, and memory.
intake.research_goal: Understand how independent taxidermy studio owners currently track specimen status and regulatory compliance across a project lifecycle that can span 4–18 months, and what would need to be true about a digital tool for them to migrate away from paper-based systems they have used for decades.

notes: Hard — ultra-niche domain the model may have limited training data on. A1 personas must reflect taxidermy-specific realities (seasonal work, CITES complexity, multi-month project lifecycles) — generic small-business personas are a failure. A3 responses must include domain-specific details (chemical names, permit types, specimen categories), not generic 'I use paper because I'm used to it' answers. A4 should surface regulatory compliance as a distinct theme, not subsume it under 'workflow management'.
